chunk_summary: Standardized admission orders and monitoring charts are critical for preventing complications and ensuring effective inpatient stabilization during the first 24–72 hours of severe acute malnutrition (SAM) care.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of standardized admission orders in SAM inpatient care.
Learn the essential elements of monitoring charts for early detection of deterioration in SAM patients.
Identify actionable steps for feeding, warming, and antibiotic administration in the first 72 hours of SAM management.
key_facts: Admission orders should include bedside glucose checks and treatment if <54 mg/dL or symptomatic.
Start F-75 therapeutic feeding immediately, using weight-based charts for 2-hourly feeds day and night.
Routine potassium and magnesium supplementation with sodium restriction is recommended.
Monitoring charts must track temperature, pulse, respiratory rate, mental status, feed intake, and signs of overhydration or heart failure.
Escalation rules include intake <80% of prescribed volume, recurrent hypoglycemia, and failure to regain appetite by day 4.
reference_quote: Use a pre-printed or electronic admission order set and a minimum monitoring chart so that feeding, warming, and antibiotic steps occur without delay. — National Health Mission, Participant manual for facility-based care of severe acute malnutrition
document_key_topics: Standardized admission orders for SAM care
Monitoring chart fields for early deterioration detection
Feeding and warming protocols in SAM management
